retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Fluoride Release
retrieved_docs.content: Various forms of fluorides (CaF 2 , SrF 2 , LaF 2 , Na 3 AlF 6 , AlF 3 ) were added to the powder of GIC during manufacture as a flux, and their antibacterial properties were observed later. In completely set cement, fluoride ions are not an essential part of the matrix and are in an unbound form. These ions are released into the saliva by diffusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Aerospace Operational Mishap Forensic Considerations -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Military crews work with the flight surgeon, and civilians must work with local law enforcement (per 14 CFR section 91.17) to obtain drug and alcohol testing following the applicable military or FAA and DOT instructions. [6] For military investigations, specimens include 14 mL of blood in 2 sodium fluoride (gray-top) tubes, 7 mL in 2 sodium EDTA (purple-top) tubes, and 50 to 70 mL of urine from each crew member. Sample collection should occur immediately as the metabolism of ingested substances continues. The testing that is typically accomplished is looking for the presence of volatile compounds such as ethanol and its breakdown products, drugs of abuse, and synthetic compounds such as spice, as discussed in the U.S. Department of Transportation FAA Aircraft Accident and Incident Notification, Investigation, and Reporting Order 8020.11D and the Armed Forces Medical Examiners System (AFMES) Division of Forensic toxicology’s “Guidelines for collecting and shipment of toxicology specimens” from 2015. If the mishap caused fatalities, work with the local qualified medical examiner (civilian) or the Armed Forces Medical Examiner (military) to guide moving/examining human remains. Consider examining and photographing all aircrew flight equipment for evidence of the system's effective or ineffective use and performance. Reference FAA Order 8020.11D - Aircraft Accident and Incident Notification, Investigation, and Reporting, AFI 91-204, AFMAN 91-223.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrofluoric Acid Burns -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The Major source of production of hydrofluoric acid is by treating fluorite with concentrated sulfuric acid at temperatues of 265 degrees fahrenheit to produce hydrofluic acid and calcium sulfate.  Alternate production is a by-product in the production of phosphoric acid from the mineral apatite.  Hydrofluoric acid is also produced by the release from industrial and welding processes and environmental activities such as volcanoes.  HF production exceeds one million tons world wide by a number of manufactors and is transported and stored under high pressure as a highly concentrated liquid. [7] In the United States of America, the Department of Transportation Hazard Label and National Fire Protection Assocation (NFPA) 704 lists the health value of 4 (can be lethal), flammability value of 0 (will not burn under typical fire conditions), instability value of 2 (readily undergoes violent chamical changes at elevated temperatures and pressures) and special value of W with line through (reacts violently or exposevely with water).  Domestic concentrations of hydrofluoric acid is typically around 0.5% with industrial concentrations approaching 100%.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrofluoric Acid Burns -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrofluoric acid was discovered in 1771 by Swedish pharmaceutical chemist Carl Wilhelm Scheele when he was investigating the mineral called fluorite (Calcium fluoride).  Hydrogen fluoride (HF) has several synonyms: Hydrofluoric acid, Fluoric acid, Hydrofluoride, Fluorine monohydride, Fluorane.  Hydrofluoric (HF) acid is an extremely powerful inorganic acid and a vigorous dehydrating agent that is used in many industrial branches including production of aluminum, stainless steel and hydrofluorocarbons, glass etching, stevedoring and transportation industries, inorganic and organic chemical manufacturing, mineral processing, petroleum oil refineries, fire extinguishers manufacturing, steel mills, cleaning HVAC systems, and as the precursor to all fluorine compands in the pharmaceutical industry. [1] [2] [3] [4] Consumer products containing hydrofluoric acid include rust removers, detergents, marble, brick and stone cleaning, toilet bowl clearners, insecticides, automobile wheel cleaners and air conditioner cleaners. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Fluorocarbons are fluorinated carbon chain polymers that are used in an assortment of household and commercial products as waterproofing agents, lubricants, sealants, and leather conditioners [1] .

retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: These products have been implicated in sporadic outbreaks of respiratory illness characterized by dyspnea, cough, and chest pain [2] .  Though typically self-limited, more severe exposure may lead to respiratory failure, acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), and even death.  Studies investigating the mechanism of fluoropolymer-related respiratory illness in humans have been difficult to perform for the following reasons:  chemical formulation of these products is proprietary, chemical formulations are intermittently reformulated, product labels and safety data sheets (SDS) may not report fluorocarbon presence if present only in small amounts [3] .

retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Fluorocarbons are fluorinated carbon chain polymers that are used in an assortment of household and commercial products as waterproofing agents, lubricants, sealants, and leather conditioners. These products have been implicated in sporadic outbreaks of respiratory illness characterized by dyspnea, cough, and chest pain. Though typically self-limited, more severe exposure may lead to respiratory failure, acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), and even death. Studies investigating the mechanism of fluoropolymer-related respiratory illness in humans have been difficult to perform for the following reasons: chemical formulation of these products is proprietary, chemical formulations are intermittently reformulated, product labels and safety data sheets (SDS) may not report fluorocarbon presence if present only in small amounts. This activity reviews the etiology, presentation, evaluation, and management/prevention of fluoride/fluorocarbon toxicity, and reviews the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating, diagnosing, and managing the condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Fluoride Release
retrieved_docs.content: Two mechanisms have been proposed to explain the fluoride release from GIC into an aqueous solution. [12]  The first mechanism is a short-term process involving rapidly dissolving fluorides from the outer surface into the solution. [12] The second mechanism is a continuous and gradual diffusion of fluoride through the cement.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Because these products have been associated with multiple occurrences of respiratory illness, with hundreds of victims involved in some outbreaks, fluoropolymer-related respiratory illness has become a public health concern. Despite this, a review of the literature on this topic fails to yield a clearly elucidated mechanism to explain why illness develops after exposure to some products and not others.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoropolymers are ultrafine particles ranging in size from 0.02-0.2 µm [5] .  These particles can aggregate and produce particle agglomerates measuring up to 15um [6] [7] . This increased particle size is thought to occur with product aging and has been associated with attenuation of toxicity [6] .

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Fluorosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The most common route of fluoride ingestion is via drinking water. [3] Alternative sources include plants, which similarly obtain fluorides through water uptake. Fluorides can also be inhaled. This occupational hazard is associated with mining and smelting metals, including aluminum. [5] Equally, fluoride deficiency is associated with deleterious effects, most notably an increased vulnerability to dental caries. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the basic pathophysiology and toxicokinetics of fluorocarbon exposure toxicity. Identify the common presenting features of fluoride and fluorocarbon toxicity on an examination/evaluation. Outline the treatment and management strategies available for patients with fluoride/fluorocarbon toxicity. Summarize how an optimally functioning interprofessional team would coordinate care to enhance outcomes for patients suffering from fluorocarbon toxicity. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluorides and Fluorocarbons Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most convincing evidence to date regarding a mechanism of disease is the change in fluoropolymer structure (from fluoroalkane to fluoroalkene) [3] .  It is theorized that this structural change in conjunction with patient-specific factors (metabolic activation, smoking, preexisting medical conditions, etc.) is most likely to cause the development of illness. Other investigations have reported an association between the occurrence of outbreaks and changes in particle size, particle emission rate, and changes in the propellants or solvents. The presence of multiple different reformulations may explain the varying reports.

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Issues of Concern -- Fluoride Release
retrieved_docs.content: The bulk of the GIC matrix plays a vital role in the fluoride release capacity. The fluoride release is influenced by intrinsic factors such as the composition of the matrix, setting mechanism, and fluoride content, and extrinsic factors like the pH of saliva, plaque, pellicle formation, liquid-to-powder ratio, mixing, setting and curing time, and the amount of exposed surface. Bleaching and tooth brushing do not significantly affect fluoride release. [13] The coverage of the surface of GIC with an adhesive or surface protective agent results in a 1.4 to 4-fold decrease in fluoride release.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Testing Procedures -- Filtration
retrieved_docs.content: Depth filtration follows a similar mechanism as surface filtration, but the particles are retained on the surface and the body of the filter. The fibers network is arranged in such a manner that they retain bi particles. Examples include earthenware (Berkfield and Chamberland), asbestos (Seitz), sintered glass, and cellulose membrane.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1297
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements are chemically related to fluorides and are classified as a type of fluorine compounds, indicating that they belong to the broader group of fluorine compounds while maintaining a direct relationship with fluorides. This connection clarifies the composition of fluoride supplements and their place within the category of fluorine‑based substances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 433
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements are classified as fluorine compounds and are specifically associated with fluorides, indicating that they belong to the broader group of chemical agents containing fluorine. Fluorocholine is also categorized as a fluorine compound, placing it in the same chemical family as fluoride supplements. This relationship underscores that both fluoride supplements and fluorocholine share the common characteristic of being fluorine‑based substances, which is relevant for understanding their pharmacological properties and potential uses in medical treatment or diagnostic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: adverse‑event reporting and clinical management. Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1987
retrieved_docs.content: The calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, sodium fluoride combination is linked to the compound known in Czech as fluorid sodný, indicating that this multi‑ingredient formulation is derived from or mapped from fluorid sodný. Fluorid sodný itself is identified as having the specific form sodium fluoride F‑18, a radioactive isotope of sodium fluoride. Consequently, the combination of calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride can be understood as a preparation that incorporates the properties of sodium fluoride F‑18, reflecting its origin from fluorid sodný.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1966
retrieved_docs.content: The formulation that combines calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride is directly linked to sodium fluoride (fluorid sodný) and is regarded as being derived or mapped from that fluoride component, emphasizing that the multi‑ingredient mixture is fundamentally based on the properties of sodium fluoride.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 722
retrieved_docs.content: The combination of calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride is derived from sodium fluoride (fluorid sodný), indicating that this multi‑ingredient preparation is mapped from the basic sodium fluoride compound. Fluoride supplements are classified as members of the fluorid sodný group, meaning they are formulations that contain sodium fluoride as their active ingredient. Sodium fluoride (fluorid sodný) also exists in a specific isotopic form known as sodium fluoride F‑18, which is used in diagnostic imaging. In addition, fluoride supplements have a relationship with fluoridy, signifying that they provide fluoride ions to the body. Together, these connections illustrate how calcium and magnesium salts combined with sodium fluoride form the basis of fluoride supplementation, how such supplements belong to the broader sodium fluoride category, and how sodium fluoride can be presented in the F‑18 isotopic form for clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1988
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements are linked to the concept of fluoridy, indicating a relationship between the use of supplemental fluoride and the process or effect denoted by fluoridy. This connection highlights how fluoride supplementation may influence or be associated with fluoridy‑related outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1345
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements are classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as a subgroup of other mineral supplements. In addition to their classification, fluoride supplements are linked to the concept of fluoridy, indicating a specific relationship or relevance to fluoride-related effects or actions. This dual characterization highlights both their therapeutic category and their functional association with fluoride.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 363
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements, selenium supplements, and zinc supplements are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) category of “other mineral supplements,” indicating that they are used to correct or prevent deficiencies of essential trace elements. Fluoride supplements are specifically linked to fluoridy, reflecting their role in providing fluoride for dental health and bone metabolism. Zinc supplements are further identified as members of zinc gluconate, a particular zinc salt formulation commonly employed to improve zinc bioavailability. Recognizing that these three agents belong to the same ATC subclass helps clinicians consider them together when addressing mineral deficiencies, while also acknowledging the distinct therapeutic actions of fluoride (fluoridy) and the specific formulation of zinc gluconate within zinc supplementation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2125
retrieved_docs.content: Radionuclide imaging – action is identified as a method of other nuclear medicine imaging, indicating that this imaging technique is employed as one of the various approaches within the broader field of nuclear medicine to visualize physiological and biochemical processes in patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 173
retrieved_docs.content: demonstrate that sodium‑based compounds function both as excipients in systemic drugs and as active or supplemental agents in dental therapeutics, while mineral supplements (including zinc gluconate) and certain fluorine‑based radiopharmaceuticals are organized within the ATC framework according to their chemical composition and therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 895
retrieved_docs.content: 0.0088 MG/MG Toothpaste. Across these preparations, sodium monofluorophosphate provides fluoride ions that inhibit demineralization and promote remineralization of tooth enamel, thereby reducing the incidence of dental caries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1043
retrieved_docs.content: for reflux‑related gastrointestinal disorders, while sodium monofluorophosphate provides the fluoride activity in a wide range of oral care products. Understanding these interconnections assists clinicians, pharmacists and formulators in product selection, anticipation of excipient effects, and appropriate therapeutic use of calcium carbonate.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 34
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic radioisotopes are a specialized subset of radiofarmaka—radiopharmaceutical agents used for diagnostic imaging—and encompass a wide array of radionuclide compounds that enable organ‑specific imaging, bone scanning, renal function assessment, and tumor localization. Representative members include 99mTc‑technecistan sodný, carbon‑14 (a parent isotope for carbon‑containing labeled products), potassium‑42 and potassium‑43 incorporated into potassium‑chloride, indium‑111 and its clinical formulation indium‑111 oxyquinoline, colloidal sulfur labeled with technetium‑99m, sodium fluoride labeled with fluorine‑18 (NaF F‑18), sodium iodide I‑123, and numerous technetium‑99m–based agents such as technetium‑99m dimeric captopril acid, technetium‑99m disofenin, technetium‑99m exametazim, technetium‑99m lidofenin, technetium‑99m medronate, and technetium‑99m‑aggregated albumin. These isotopes often generate downstream “child” labeled compounds that extend their clinical utility across nuclear medicine. In dental preventive therapy, fluoride agents—tin fluoride (fluorid cínatý), sodium fluoride (fluorid sodný), and the organic fluoride Olaflur—play a central role in enamel remineralization; a formulation combining calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride maps to sodium fluoride, highlighting its importance in prophylaxis. The radiolabeled form NaF F‑18 links dental prophylaxis to nuclear imaging by permitting quantitative evaluation of bone and dental mineral metabolism. Together, the interrelated concepts of diagnostic radioisotopes (including carbon‑14, technetium‑99m derivatives, potassium and indium isotopes) and fluoride compounds illustrate a cohesive framework that supports integrated clinical decision‑making and optimized patient management across radiology, nuclear medicine, and dental care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1898
retrieved_docs.content: Fluorocholine is classified as a fluorine compound, indicating that its chemical structure contains fluorine atoms and it belongs to the broader group of sloučeniny fluoru.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 987
retrieved_docs.content: The combination product containing calcium carbonate, calcium phosphate, magnesium hydroxide, and sodium fluoride is conceptually derived from the sodium‑fluoride entity (fluorid sodný). Fluoride supplements are classified as members of the sodium‑fluoride group, are described as having a fluoride (fluoridy) relationship, and are identified as a type of “other mineral supplements in ATC,” the same ATC category that includes selenium supplement. Within the sodium‑fluoride classification, a specific form exists known as sodium fluoride F‑18. Together, these relationships show how various mineral‑based formulations—ranging from multi‑component calcium and magnesium salts combined with sodium fluoride to single‑ingredient fluoride and selenium supplements—are organized under the broader ATC grouping of other mineral supplements, highlighting their shared pharmacological context and classification.
